title: Introduction to Citizenship
section1Text: Imagine you live in an apartment building. If everyone leaves trash in the stairs, plays loud music at 2 AM, or takes the elevator without letting elderly people pass, life becomes miserable. Citizenship is precisely the set of rules (written or unwritten) that allow us to live together harmoniously.
section2Text: Citizenship is the fact of belonging to a political community (a country, a city, a region) and participating in its life. It provides rights (freedoms) but also implies duties (obligations).
section3: Difference between citizenship and nationality – THE TRAP TO AVOID!
section3Text:
Nationality: This is a legal bond between you and a State. You can have Malagasy nationality without ever voting or helping your neighbor. Example: a newborn baby in Madagascar has Malagasy nationality but is not yet an active citizen.
Citizenship: This is active participation. You are a citizen when you exercise your rights (voting, expressing opinions) and fulfill your duties (paying taxes, respecting laws).
mnemonic: Nationality = Identity card. Citizenship = Active member card.
section4Text:
- Civil citizenship: relationships between individuals (respect, politeness, solidarity). Example: not insulting your classmate.
- Political citizenship: relationship to the State (voting, running for office, demonstrating). Example: voting at 18.
- Social citizenship: relationship to the community (helping the poor, protecting the environment). Example: donating clothes to disaster victims.
section5Text: The goal of this course is not just to make you memorize definitions. It is to prepare you to become a responsible adult, capable of understanding the world and taking action to make it better. A good citizen does not just know all the laws; they respect others and participate.
question: Can we be a good citizen without ever voting? Why?

title: Rights, Duties, and Responsibilities
section1Text: Imagine a football game. If nobody respects the rules, it turns into a fight and the match stops. In society, it's the exact same. Rights (the power to shoot at the goal) and duties (not committing fouls) allow the game to continue.
section2: The 5 fundamental rights of a citizen (to remember)
section2Text:
1. Right to life: nobody can legally kill you (with rare exceptions).
2. Right to freedom: you can move around, think, and express yourself (within the limits of the law).
3. Right to education: primary school is free and compulsory.
4. Right to work: you have the right to seek employment and not be discriminated against.
5. Right to vote: at 18, you can choose your representatives.
section3: The 5 duties of a citizen (not to forget)
section3Text:
1. Respect the law: do not steal, obey traffic regulations.
2. Pay taxes: this money is used to pay for roads, schools, and hospitals.
3. Respect others: reject harassment and insults.
4. Protect the environment: do not litter, conserve water and energy.
5. Defend your country: respond to the army's call if called upon.
mnemonic: Life, Liberty, Education, Work, Vote -> L.L.E.W.V.
section4Text: Responsibility means accepting the consequences of what you do. If you break a window, you pay for it. If you lie, people stop believing you. A responsible citizen does not shift the blame onto others.
section5Text: Lucas and Kanto are in high school. Lucas studies every evening (responsibility) and passes his exams. Kanto parties all year, does not do his homework, and fails (irresponsibility). In adult life, the consequences of our choices are even more critical.
question: Which duty is the most difficult to respect in your opinion, and why?

title: The Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR)
section1: Why does this text exist? (The hook)
section1Text: In 1939-1945, World War II killed millions of people. The Nazis massacred populations in horrifying ways. After this tragedy, world leaders said: 'We need a text that protects all human beings, everywhere, forever.' This is how the UDHR was born.
section2Text: Adopted on December 10, 1948 by the UN (United Nations). Each year, we celebrate this historical date as International Human Rights Day.
section3Text:
Article 1: All human beings are born free and equal in dignity and rights.
Article 3: Everyone has the right to life, liberty, and security of person.
Article 5: No one shall be subjected to torture or cruel treatment.
Article 11: Presumption of innocence (innocent until proven guilty).
Article 26: Everyone has the right to free primary education.
mnemonic: 1 = Freedom, 3 = Life, 5 = No torture, 11 = Innocent, 26 = School
section4Text: Even if some countries still violate these rights, the UDHR serves as a global foundation for judging dictators, protecting refugees, and defending women and minorities against abuse.
section5Text: Although the Declaration is not legally binding on its own, it forms the basis of international human rights law and inspires many national constitutions.
question: Which article of the UDHR seems the most threatened today?

title: Corruption
section1: The story of the police officer (The hook)
section1Text: A taxi driver is speeding. A police officer stops him. Normally, the fine is 50,000 FCFA. But the officer says: 'Give me 20,000 FCFA now, and I'll let you go.' This is a bribe. It is corruption.
section2Text: Corruption is the misuse of power or entrusted authority (public office, position) to obtain an unfair personal benefit (money, gifts, favors).
section3Text:
- Bribe: giving money to bypass rules.
- Nepotism: unduly favoring family members.
- Favouritism: giving special privileges to friends.
- Embezzlement: stealing public funds meant for the community.
- Fraud & Extortion: cheating or intimidating to extort money.
section4Text:
- Injustice: the rich get richer, the poor stay poor.
- Poverty: money that was supposed to build a school ends up in a corrupt person's pocket.
- Economic slowdown and Loss of trust: foreign investors flee, and citizens lose trust in the state.
section5Text: Educate children from a young age, punish corrupt individuals severely (prison, heavy fines), and make all public accounts transparent and open to citizens.
question: How can a young person take action against corruption on a daily basis?

title: Gender, Discrimination, and Violence
section1Text: Why do people say girls must like makeup and boys must like car mechanics? These are not biological facts, but social constructions. Distinguishing biology from social expectations helps fight stereotypes.
section2Text:
Sex: boy or girl (biological characteristics present at birth).
Gender: roles, behaviors, and expectations that society attributes to each sex (e.g., 'girls like pink, boys like blue' -> this is a social stereotype).
section3: Discrimination – when you treat someone differently
section3Text: Treating a person unfairly because of their sex, race/color, religion, age, or disability. Example: Refusing to hire a qualified woman because she 'might have children' is illegal.
section4: The 4 types of violence to know
section4Text:
1. Physical: hitting, kicking, bodily harm.
2. Psychological: constant humiliation, harassment, social exclusion.
3. Verbal: insults, name-calling, threats.
4. Sexual: forced sexual acts or touching without consent.
section5: How to fight discrimination and violence
section5Text: At school, report any abuse to teachers or counselors. In the family, speak to a trusted adult. By law, file a complaint to punish the offender.
question: Have you ever witnessed discrimination? How did you react?

title: Sustainable Development
section1Text: If we cut down all the trees in a forest today to manufacture paper, what will be left tomorrow for our children to breathe and find shelter? Sustainable development means 'meeting the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own'.
section2Text:
- Economic: creating sustainable wealth and jobs without exhausting resources.
- Social: ensuring justice, health, and education for everyone.
- Environmental: protecting the balance of nature, air, water, and climate.
mnemonic: Pyramid with 3 faces: Economic, Social, Environmental. If one falls, the whole pyramid collapses.
section3Text: Climate change triggers severe heatwaves and floods. Natural resources are rapidly depleting, and inequalities are widening, heavily impacting the most vulnerable countries.
section4Text: Planting trees, recycling waste (plastic, paper, glass), turning off lights when leaving, saving water by turning off taps, and using public transport or bicycles.
section5Text: The United Nations defined 17 Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) to eradicate poverty, protect the planet, and ensure global peace by 2030.
question: What eco-friendly small steps do you already practice at home or at school?

title: Solidarity and Citizen Participation
section1Text: A storm destroys your neighbor's roof. Alone, they will take months to repair it. If the whole neighborhood helps, the roof is rebuilt in a weekend. This is solidarity: joining forces in the face of difficulties.
section2Text: It is voluntary mutual assistance between members of a community. Examples: collecting food for charity, donating blood, or helping an elderly person carry their groceries.
section3Text:
- Voting in elections once you reach the age of majority (18).
- Engaging actively in local community associations.
- Participating in neighborhood or public debates.
- Reporting municipal issues (broken street lights, potholes) to the town hall.
mnemonic: Solidarity = Heart. Participation = Hands of action.
section4Text: Volunteers work without financial pay. They freely share their time. In return, they receive invaluable gifts: gratitude, sincere smiles, and the profound pride of making the world a better place.
section5Text: Cleaning up a beach or local forest, planting urban community gardens, or offer free school tutoring to pupils in need.
question: What volunteer work would you do if you had 2 hours of spare time per week?
